jokes.text: This bus has seen better days. Well, at least it's safer than the old bus.
jokes.setup: Comment about the current bus being in poor condition
jokes.punchline: Revelation that somehow this terrible bus is actually an improvement
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective setup/payoff establishing the school's dire financial situation through understated irony.

jokes.text: Time to move. The hole's getting bigger.
jokes.setup: Students commenting on bus condition
jokes.punchline: Implication that the bus floor is literally falling apart with a hole
jokes.explanation: Escalates the bus deterioration joke with visual absurdity and economy of language.

jokes.text: Seymour, the children are playing in the hole again. Shouldn't you get that fixed? Edna, you know they just cut the school's budget. If I had the money, I'd fix the exhaust leak. I think it's causing some of our low test scores.
jokes.setup: Discussion about fixing a hole where children play
jokes.punchline: Skinner's casual connection between exhaust poisoning and poor academic performance
jokes.explanation: Dark humor with perfect character integration - Skinner's bureaucratic tone treating child endangerment as a minor administrative concern.

jokes.text: But it's a good thing we're not firing it because it happens to be aimed at the main support leg of that lookout tower.
jokes.setup: Civil War cannon is fully restored and ready to fire
jokes.punchline: Revelation of dangerous target alignment
jokes.explanation: Good setup for inevitable disaster, with proper tension building through specific dangerous details.

jokes.text: People don't realize that these cannons are very sensitive and the slightest jolt could set them off. Of course, for safety reasons we don't keep the cannon loaded. It's just common sense.
jokes.setup: Tour guide emphasizing cannon sensitivity and safety
jokes.punchline: Contradictory information suggesting it's actually loaded despite 'safety reasons'
jokes.explanation: Classic ironic misdirection with proper setup for the coming disaster sequence.

jokes.text: And a mint for afterwards. Five dollars a child? Last year it was free! New ownership.
jokes.setup: Otto getting gas and mints, then shock at admission price increase
jokes.punchline: Simple explanation of 'new ownership' for dramatic price hike
jokes.explanation: Realistic observation about tourist trap economics with good economy of language.

jokes.text: Here's the admission, plus something for you. See that they get a little extra education. Yes, sir, Principal Valiant! He thinks he's so hot ever since he swept the Princi Awards. Those things are rigged.
jokes.setup: Rival principal showing off with bribe for extra education
jokes.punchline: Skinner's jealous dismissal of Princi Awards as rigged
jokes.explanation: Perfect Skinner characterization - petty jealousy disguised as principled objection, with absurd 'Princi Awards' detail.

jokes.text: On May 21, 1864, the men of the 9th Bearded Infantry were sunning and fluffing their beards in the sun.
jokes.setup: Civil War historical narration
jokes.punchline: Ridiculous military unit name and vain grooming activities
jokes.explanation: Highly original absurdist take on Civil War history with memorable phrasing that subverts expectations.

jokes.text: Fort Springfield, we surrender unconditionally! We're sick! We need leeches and hacksaws to saw off our gangrenous limbs. But the Springfield brigade was too brave to accept their surrender.
jokes.setup: Enemy troops surrendering with medical emergency
jokes.punchline: Springfield being 'too brave' to accept surrender from sick people
jokes.explanation: Dark irony reframing cowardly massacre as heroic bravery, with graphic medical details adding absurdity.

jokes.text: Come on, boys. Those white flags are no match for our muskets! Charge! And the Springfielders heroically slaughtered their enemies as they prayed for mercy.
jokes.setup: Military charge against surrendering enemies
jokes.punchline: Describing massacre of praying enemies as 'heroic'
jokes.explanation: Continuation of dark irony about sanitized historical narratives, effective juxtaposition of 'heroically' with 'slaughtered.'

jokes.text: It's hard to see what's going on. I can only make out the fat soldiers.
jokes.setup: Student trying to see Civil War reenactment
jokes.punchline: Can only distinguish soldiers by body type
jokes.explanation: Good child-like observation with economy, implies visual of overweight reenactors.

jokes.text: Hey, they're trying to learn for free!
jokes.setup: Students trying to see educational content
jokes.punchline: Treating education as theft
jokes.explanation: Sharp satire on commercialization of education with perfect economy and quotable phrasing.

jokes.text: Get them! Use your phony guns as clubs! Run, children. Start the bus, Otto! Start the bus! Damn! I shouldn't have eaten the mint first.
jokes.setup: Chase scene with reenactors attacking children
jokes.punchline: Otto's regret about eating the mint affecting his escape driving
jokes.explanation: Good escalation with callback to earlier mint detail, adding character-specific obstacle to escape.

jokes.text: Seymour, because of your penny-pinching we're coming back from a field trip with the fewest children yet.
jokes.setup: Criticism of Skinner's budgeting
jokes.punchline: Casual mention of losing children on field trips as routine occurrence
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark humor with perfect economy - implies regular child endangerment as bureaucratic statistic.

jokes.text: God bless the man who invented permission slips.
jokes.setup: Edna's criticism about losing children
jokes.punchline: Skinner's grateful relief about legal protection
jokes.explanation: Perfect Skinner moment - bureaucratic relief trumping child safety concerns. Economical and perfectly in character.

jokes.text: I think I got your lunch. I didn't think this was for me.
jokes.setup: Students examining lunches
jokes.punchline: Implication that school lunch is so bad it's mistakable for something else
jokes.explanation: Standard school lunch joke with minimal craft, but serves story function about budget cuts.

jokes.text: I don't care what you say, I can taste the newspaper. Shredded newspapers add much-needed roughage and essential inks.
jokes.setup: Complaint about food quality
jokes.punchline: Skinner's defense of newspaper as nutritional supplement
jokes.explanation: Escalates food quality joke with absurd nutritional justification, perfect bureaucratic doublespeak from Skinner.

jokes.text: Besides, you didn't notice the old gym mats. There's very little meat in these gym mats.
jokes.setup: Defense of newspaper in food
jokes.punchline: Casual admission of gym mats in food with reassuring 'very little meat'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation with perfect economy - the phrase 'very little meat' implies there IS some meat, making it even more disturbing.

jokes.text: Oh, come on, Edna! We both know these children have no future!
jokes.setup: Edna talking about children's futures
jokes.punchline: Skinner's brutally honest assessment
jokes.explanation: Dark but perfectly in character for Skinner's pessimistic worldview, good setup for next beat.

jokes.text: Prove me wrong, kids. Prove me wrong.
jokes.setup: Skinner's harsh assessment of children's futures
jokes.punchline: Challenging students to prove their worth while undermining them
jokes.explanation: Great follow-up beat showing Skinner's self-awareness of his pessimism, delivered with characteristic deadpan.

jokes.text: Strike, eh? My bones are so brittle. But I always drink plenty of 'malk'?
jokes.setup: Student commenting on potential strike
jokes.punchline: Mispronouncing 'milk' while complaining about brittle bones
jokes.explanation: Memorable mispronunciation joke suggesting poor education quality, became iconic Simpsons moment.

jokes.text: This pointer's so old, it's worn down to a nub. It still points, doesn't it?
jokes.setup: Teacher complaining about worn-down pointer
jokes.punchline: Skinner's dismissive practical response
jokes.explanation: Solid character moment showing Skinner's penny-pinching rationalization of inadequate supplies.

jokes.text: The only books we have are ones that were banned by other schools. Well, the kids have to learn about Tek War sooner or later.
jokes.setup: Complaint about banned books being their only materials
jokes.punchline: Skinner treating sci-fi schlock as essential curriculum
jokes.explanation: Excellent specific pop culture reference (William Shatner's Tek War novels) with perfect deadpan delivery suggesting educational necessity.

jokes.text: I saw you two fighting. I'm worried there could be a strike and the school would shut down. I'm sure you'd really hate that.
jokes.setup: Bart expressing concern about school closure
jokes.punchline: Obvious insincerity about hating school closure
jokes.explanation: Standard Bart manipulation with transparent insincerity, sets up his scheme well.

jokes.text: Skinner said you wouldn't go through with it. Well, I had to clean it up a little. But basically, Krabappel said you'd give the teachers everything they want. She did? Yeah. She said you'd fold faster than Superman on laundry day.
jokes.setup: Bart playing both sides with false quotes
jokes.punchline: Absurd Superman laundry metaphor
jokes.explanation: Brilliant unexpected metaphor combining Superman's speed with mundane laundry task, highly quotable and original.

jokes.text: Simpson, I always thought you were sneaky and manipulative. Now I see you're really a very sensitive little boy.
jokes.setup: Skinner's assessment of Bart
jokes.punchline: Completely wrong interpretation of Bart's obvious manipulation
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Skinner's naivety about student manipulation despite being initially correct.

jokes.text: That's it! Strike!
jokes.setup: Mounting tension between administration and teachers
jokes.punchline: Edna's breaking point declaration
jokes.explanation: Functional plot moment with good economy, satisfying payoff to building tension.

jokes.text: I'd like to get down now.
jokes.setup: Implied student is stuck somewhere due to chaos
jokes.punchline: Understated request for help
jokes.explanation: Simple understated request suggesting student abandonment during strike chaos.

jokes.text: Hey, now that Largo's gone, we can play the forbidden music.
jokes.setup: Students noting teacher's absence
jokes.punchline: Existence of 'forbidden music' in music class
jokes.explanation: Good concept that music class would have forbidden pieces, suggests authoritarian teaching style.

jokes.text: I heard that!
jokes.setup: Students thinking teacher is gone
jokes.punchline: Immediate contradiction from still-present teacher
jokes.explanation: Perfect timing and economy for classical 'teacher still there' beat.

jokes.text: Great news, Mom! Horrible news, Mom! The school's on strike! Maybe forever. Overload. Pleasure overload.
jokes.setup: Bart and Lisa's opposing reactions to strike
jokes.punchline: Bart's overwhelming joy at permanent school closure
jokes.explanation: Perfect character contrast between studious Lisa and school-hating Bart, 'pleasure overload' adds comedic intensity.

jokes.text: Overload. Pleasure overload.
jokes.setup: Learning school might be closed forever
jokes.punchline: Bart's theatrical expression of extreme joy
jokes.explanation: Good Bart character moment - over-the-top celebration of educational disaster

jokes.text: Sit up straight. Eyes forward. No talking. Is that gum? Is that gum? Is that gum?
jokes.setup: Marge taking control as substitute teacher/parent
jokes.punchline: Repetitive authoritarian questioning
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Marge's transformation into strict teacher mode, repetitive questioning builds tension.

jokes.text: Bart, leave that crowbar here. You know I don't like you prying and jimmying.
jokes.setup: Bart attempting to leave with crowbar
jokes.punchline: Marge's specific terminology about criminal activities
jokes.explanation: Excellent Marge moment using proper terms for breaking and entering while treating it as normal parenting issue.

jokes.text: You, at the crane! Spin around real fast! Now, you! Dump 3 tons of sand onto that Porta-Potty.
jokes.setup: Bart giving construction orders
jokes.punchline: Increasingly elaborate and destructive commands
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Bart's mischief during unsupervised time, specific construction details add realism.

jokes.text: Hey! Can't you tell my voice from a 10-year-old kid's?
jokes.setup: Construction worker following Bart's orders
jokes.punchline: Belated realization of authority source
jokes.explanation: Good payoff to construction sequence, worker's delayed recognition adds believable confusion.

jokes.text: Hey, that little boy is playing three games at once. Checkmate. Checkmate. Checkmate. Dang.
jokes.setup: Observer noting Milhouse's multitasking
jokes.punchline: Milhouse winning multiple chess games simultaneously then losing
jokes.explanation: Good characterization showing Milhouse's hidden intellectual abilities and typical failure pattern.

jokes.text: Lousy teachers, trying to palm off our kids on us. Dad, by striking, they're trying to change management so they can be happier and more productive. Lisa, if you don't like your job, you don't strike. You just go in every day and do it really half-assed. That's the American way.
jokes.setup: Homer complaining about teacher strike, Lisa's reasoned response
jokes.punchline: Homer's philosophy about workplace dissatisfaction
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer wisdom - completely wrong but internally consistent. Brilliant satire of American work ethic with quotable delivery.

jokes.text: Relax? I can't relax! Nor can I yield, relent or... Only two synonyms? Oh, my God! I'm losing my perspicacity!
jokes.setup: Lisa stressed about missing school
jokes.punchline: Panic over limited vocabulary, using advanced word while claiming loss
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Lisa characterization - the irony of using 'perspicacity' while claiming to lose vocabulary, perfect intellectual anxiety.

jokes.text: Well, it's always in the last place you look.
jokes.setup: Lisa panicking about losing her perspicacity
jokes.punchline: Homer's unhelpful literal truism
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer logic - technically correct but completely missing the point, good contrast to Lisa's panic.

jokes.text: Good news, people! I'm happy to announce another union has joined us in a sympathy strike! The Piano Tuners Local 412!
jokes.setup: Announcement of union solidarity
jokes.punchline: Extremely specific and irrelevant union support
jokes.explanation: Absurdist humor with perfect specificity - piano tuners have no connection to school issues but union solidarity logic.

jokes.text: You know, I heard Skinner say the teachers will crack any minute. Skinner said we'll crack, purple monkey dishwasher.
jokes.setup: Bart manipulating with false quote
jokes.punchline: Complete nonsense addition shows message corruption
jokes.explanation: Brilliant demonstration of how messages get corrupted - 'purple monkey dishwasher' became iconic Simpsons absurdity.

jokes.text: Especially for that 'purple monkey dishwasher' remark.
jokes.setup: Teachers already angry at Skinner
jokes.punchline: Taking particular offense at the nonsense phrase
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation - treating gibberish as the most insulting part of the message

jokes.text: I found a hive of killer bees. You want to go throw rocks at it? Sorry, Bart. I'm deeply immersed in the Teapot Dome scandal. However, it might be feasible in a fortnight.
jokes.setup: Bart's typical dangerous activity proposal
jokes.punchline: Milhouse's scholarly response with formal scheduling
jokes.explanation: Excellent contrast between Bart's destructive impulses and Milhouse's newfound intellectualism, 'fortnight' is perfectly pretentious.

jokes.text: I can play in two weeks.
jokes.setup: Milhouse's formal 'fortnight' response
jokes.punchline: Translation to normal language
jokes.explanation: Good follow-up showing Milhouse isn't completely pretentious, knows his audience needs translation.

jokes.text: All this free time. I never thought I'd get sick of Razor Fight II, The Slashening. I probably should stop. My doctor says I have the wrists of an 80-year-old.
jokes.setup: Student enjoying too much video game time
jokes.punchline: Medical consequences from excessive gaming
jokes.explanation: Good escalation with specific medical consequence, 'The Slashening' is a nice touch for sequel naming.

jokes.text: What do you mean, the bank is out of money? Insolvent? You only have enough cash for the next three customers? Just a second here. No, no. I don't have your money here. It's in Bill's house, and Fred's house.
jokes.setup: Bank run scenario from It's a Wonderful Life
jokes.punchline: Classic movie dialogue recreated
jokes.explanation: Movie parody of classic scene, works for viewers familiar with reference but limited originality.

jokes.text: Hey, what the hell you doing with my money in your house, Fred?
jokes.setup: It's a Wonderful Life bank explanation
jokes.punchline: Customer's logical but aggressive response
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of movie parody with realistic angry customer response to banking explanation.

jokes.text: Grade me! Look at me! Evaluate and rank me! I'm good, good, good and, oh, so smart! Grade me!
jokes.setup: Lisa's academic withdrawal symptoms
jokes.punchline: Desperate pleading for academic validation
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lisa characterization showing her unhealthy dependence on academic approval, cringe but sympathetic.

jokes.text: This morning, I caught her trying to dissect her own raincoat. I know. And this perpetual-motion machine she made today is a joke. It just keeps going faster and faster.
jokes.setup: Marge concerned about Lisa's obsessive behavior
jokes.punchline: Homer dismissing functional perpetual motion machine
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Homer moment - dismissing impossible scientific achievement as 'a joke' because it works too well.

jokes.text: And this perpetual-motion machine she made today is a joke. It just keeps going faster and faster.
jokes.setup: Homer dismissing Lisa's scientific project
jokes.punchline: Describing a successful perpetual motion machine while calling it a failure
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer ignorance - dismissing impossible scientific achievement as inadequate because he doesn't understand it

jokes.text: There's something about flying a kite at night that's so unwholesome. Hello, Mother dear.
jokes.setup: Marge observing Bart's nighttime activities
jokes.punchline: Bart's unnaturally formal greeting
jokes.explanation: Good observation about nocturnal kite flying being inherently suspicious, Bart's formal address suggests guilt.

jokes.text: Hello, Mother dear.
jokes.setup: Bart returning from nighttime kite flying
jokes.punchline: Overly formal, creepy greeting suggesting he's become strange
jokes.explanation: Payoff to Marge's concern - Bart has indeed become unsettling

jokes.text: In this house, we obey the laws of thermodynamics!
jokes.setup: Homer dealing with Lisa's perpetual motion machine
jokes.punchline: Homer citing scientific principles as household rules
jokes.explanation: Iconic Simpsons moment - Homer unexpectedly citing advanced physics as parental authority. Perfect character contradiction and brilliant writing.

jokes.text: This is a dilly of a pickle. Oh, my God! The PTA has disbanded! No, no! The PTA has not disbanded.
jokes.setup: Ned's folksy assessment of the situation
jokes.punchline: Panic over PTA disbanding followed by immediate correction
jokes.explanation: Good Ned characterization with folksy language, escalation with PTA panic creates tension before quick resolution.

jokes.text: Replace teachers with superintelligent cyborgs. Or, if cyborgs aren't invented yet, use people from the neighborhood.
jokes.setup: Emergency strike plan consultation
jokes.punchline: Cyborgs as first option, neighbors as backup plan
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist humor with perfect bureaucratic tone treating impossible technology as more viable than community volunteers.

jokes.text: Talking out of turn, that's a paddling. Looking out the window, that's a paddling. Staring at my sandals, that's a paddling. Paddling the school canoe, you better believe that's a paddling.
jokes.setup: Old-fashioned substitute teacher listing infractions
jokes.punchline: Escalating to increasingly absurd paddling offenses, ending with meta-joke
jokes.explanation: Classic escalating list joke with perfect meta-punchline about paddling the paddle activity

jokes.text: Talking out of turn, that's a paddling. Looking out the window, that's a paddling. Staring at my sandals, that's a paddling. Paddling the school canoe, you better believe that's a paddling.
jokes.setup: Old-fashioned disciplinarian substitute teacher
jokes.punchline: Escalating series of paddling offenses, including recursive paddling reference
jokes.explanation: Iconic Simpsons sequence with perfect escalation and recursive humor in final paddling reference. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Yes? What is it? What? What is it? Can I play with it? No, you can't play with it. You won't enjoy it on as many levels as I do.
jokes.setup: Student asking about scientific equipment
jokes.punchline: Frink's condescending assumption about intellectual capacity
jokes.explanation: Perfect Frink characterization with academic condescension, 'on as many levels' is appropriately pretentious.

jokes.text: Anita Bath. All right. Settle down. Anita Bath here? All right, fine. Fine. Maya Buttreeks.
jokes.setup: Teacher taking attendance with prank names
jokes.punchline: Classic prank names causing classroom disruption
jokes.explanation: Standard prank name routine, functional but not particularly original or clever execution.

jokes.text: Hey, what are you laughing at? What? Oh, I get it. I get it. It's my big ears, isn't it, kids? Isn't it? Well, children, I can't help that.
jokes.setup: Teacher noticing student laughter
jokes.punchline: Misinterpreting laughter about prank names as being about his appearance
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection showing substitute's insecurity, deflecting from the real joke to personal insecurity.

jokes.text: All right. You listen up, you little freaks! The fun stops here. You're gonna shut your stinking traps and behave, damn it! This is one substitute you're not gonna screw with!
jokes.setup: New harsh substitute teacher arrival
jokes.punchline: Extremely aggressive authoritarian approach to classroom management
jokes.explanation: Good escalation after series of failed substitutes, Leopold's military-style approach provides strong contrast.

jokes.text: Hey, Bart, what about the booby trap? May I? Thank you. Mama's boy! Teacher's pet! Teacher's pet! I meant the other booby trap.
jokes.setup: Student warning about booby trap, Marge disarming one
jokes.punchline: Revelation of multiple traps, second one activating
jokes.explanation: Good setup and misdirection with Marge's politeness disarming first trap, second trap provides escalation.

jokes.text: Kids have been doing that one since my day.
jokes.setup: Marge dealing with classroom prank
jokes.punchline: Casual familiarity with decades-old prank tradition
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Marge's unexpected savviness about school pranks, suggests timeless nature.

jokes.text: Marge, anyone can miss Canada, all tucked away down there.
jokes.setup: Marge reporting students couldn't find Canada
jokes.punchline: Homer's geographical confusion about Canada's location
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer geography fail with 'tucked away down there' suggesting Canada is south of US, good economy.

jokes.text: I've had just about enough of your Vassar-bashing, young lady!
jokes.setup: Lisa mentioning not getting into Vassar
jokes.punchline: Homer's unexpected defense of elite college
jokes.explanation: Surprising Homer moment defending elite education when he typically shows no knowledge of such institutions.

jokes.text: I hate it when he gets in there.
jokes.setup: Bart escaping to crawlspace
jokes.punchline: Marge's weary familiarity with this specific problem
jokes.explanation: Good Marge moment showing exhausted parent dealing with recurring child behavior, suggests this happens regularly.

jokes.text: 'Here you go, precious.' That's a very nice jig, Kearney. Now, isn't dancing much more fun than bullying? Yeah.
jokes.setup: Student mocking Marge's maternal treatment, Marge trying to reform bully
jokes.punchline: Kearney's simple agreement to obviously leading question
jokes.explanation: Good character moments showing Marge's naive optimism about reforming bullies and Kearney's simple compliance.

jokes.text: They're like two positively charged ions. Wait, that's it. With your book smarts and my ability to exploit people with book smarts we can figure out a plan to get them back together.
jokes.setup: Milhouse's scientific analogy about incompatibility
jokes.punchline: Bart's honest assessment of their partnership dynamic
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character moment with Bart openly acknowledging his exploitation of Milhouse while Milhouse seems fine with it.

jokes.text: Yo. I used to sit right over there. It's all right, children. Just ignore the murderer. And as a bonus, some of our more troublesome students might be scared straight. Kid, help me break out of here. I'll totally make it worth your while. I'm listening.
jokes.setup: Prisoners renting classroom space, Skinner's reassurance
jokes.punchline: Immediate corruption attempt and Bart's interest
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about prison overcrowding solution, Bart's immediate criminal interest perfectly in character.

jokes.text: It's all right, children. Just ignore the murderer. And as a bonus, some of our more troublesome students... might be scared straight.
jokes.setup: Prisoners in school as budget solution
jokes.punchline: Skinner treating murderers as educational tools
jokes.explanation: Perfect Skinner rationalization - finding educational benefit in dangerous cost-cutting
